coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and application of optically stable red fluorescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of ceftazidime
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jing
Ma, Yanshun
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: This study reports the successful synthesis of optically stable red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) through a solvothermal method, using glycine as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen-doping agent. The R-CDs exhibit long-wavelength emission characteristics with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 533 nm and 600 nm, respectively, and a quantum yield of 26.7 %. The results demonstrate that R-CDs possess excellent salt resistance and photostability. The R-CDs display bright fluorescence emission and show a sensitive response to ceftazidime (CF). Leveraging these properties, a fluorescent probe based on R-CDs was developed for the sensitive determination of CF. The fluorescence quenching intensity of this system exhibits a good linear relationship with CF concentration in the range of 0–0.7 mmol/L. The linear equation is (F 0-F)/F 0=0.9564C CF(mmol/L)+0.0089, with a linear correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9945. The detection limit is 4.9 μmol/L, with recovery rates ranging from 94.2 % to 100.5 % and relative standard deviations between 2.2 % and 3.2 %. This work provides a theoretical basis for the detection of CF using red-emitting carbon dots and demonstrates promising potential for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red-emitting carbon dots
Solvothermal method
Quantum yield
Antibiotic detection
Ceftazidime detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of R-CDs. (a) TEM image, particle size distribution (bottom left inset), and HRTEM (bottom right inset); (b) FT-IR spectrum; (c) XPS survey spectrum; (d-f) C1s, N1s, and O1s fine spectra of R-CDs.

Optical characteristics of R-CDs. (a) Three-dimensional excitation-emission spectrum; (b) Two-dimensional projection; (c) UV–visible absorption spectrum (Abs), excitation spectrum (ex), and emission spectrum (em); (d) Emission spectra and (e) normalized emission spectra under different excitation wavelength.
The optical stability of R-CDs. The effects of (a) NaCl concentration, (b) UV light irradiation, (c) temperature and (d) time on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs.
Quantum yield of R-CDs. (a) Plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance; (b) Quantum yields of R-CDs in different solvents.

R-CDs as fluorescent sensors for CF detection. (a) The effects of different metal ions, antibiotics and amino acids on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs; (b) The effect of CF concentration on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs; (b) The relationship between (F 0-F)/F 0 of 600 nm emission peak and CF concentration; (d) The relationship between (F 0-F)/F 0 of 649 nm emission peak and CF concentration.

Mechanism analysis of CF detection by R-CDs (a) UV absorption spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs+CF; (b) UV absorption spectrum of CF and maximum excitation spectrum of R-CDs; (c) Fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs and R-CDs+CF.
Determination of CF content in lake water.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and application of optically stable red fluorescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of ceftazidime

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the successful synthesis of optically stable red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) through a solvothermal method, using glycine as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen-doping agent. The R-CDs exhibit long-wavelength emission characteristics with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 533 nm and 600 nm, respectively, and a quantum yield of 26.7 %. The results demonstrate that R-CDs possess excellent salt resistance and photostability. The R-CDs display bright fluorescence emission and show a sensitive response to ceftazidime (CF). Leveraging these properties, a fluorescent probe based on R-CDs was developed for the sensitive determination of CF. The fluorescence quenching intensity of this system exhibits a good linear relationship with CF concentration in the range of 0–0.7 mmol/L. The linear equation is (F 0-F)/F 0=0.9564C CF(mmol/L)+0.0089, with a linear correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9945. The detection limit is 4.9 μmol/L, with recovery rates ranging from 94.2 % to 100.5 % and relative standard deviations between 2.2 % and 3.2 %. This work provides a theoretical basis for the detection of CF using red-emitting carbon dots and demonstrates promising potential for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ceftazidime (CF) [1], a broad-spectrum third-generation cephalosporin antibiotic, is widely used in clinical treatment due to its significant efficacy against various Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. However, overuse or improper residue detection of this drug can lead to environmental issues and health risks [2]. Therefore, developing a rapid and accurate method for ceftazidime detection is crucial.

Currently, the most common methods for detecting ceftazidime include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)[3,4], ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (UPLC-Mass) [5,6], infrared spectroscopy (IR) [7,8], and HPLC with UV detection (HPLC-UV) [8,9]. These methods are favored for their high sensitivity and strong identification capabilities. However, they have drawbacks such as long operation times, high costs, and multiple sample preparation steps. Consequently, there is a pressing need for a simple, rapid, and effective method to detect ceftazidime in biological and environmental samples.

Carbon dots (CDs) [10] are relatively new carbon nanomaterials that have gained widespread attention in recent years. CDs are typically smaller than 10 nm, with a core structure mainly composed of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. They offer advantages such as high photostability [11,12], low cost, good water solubility [13,14], simple preparation processes, and excellent luminescence properties. These characteristics make CDs promising candidates for applications in solar cells [15,16], sensors [17,18], optoelectronic devices [19–21], and bioimaging [22,23]. Importantly, CDs' biocompatibility and low toxicity make them attractive for designing fluorescent probes.

In recent years, various methods have been developed to synthesize N-doped CDs, including hydrothermal [24,25], microwave [25,26], and thermal treatment [27] approaches. Fluorescent probes based on N-doped CDs have been used for antibiotic detection. However, most of these CDs emit at shorter wavelengths, potentially leading to increased background signals and affecting detection sensitivity and accuracy.

In this study, we synthesized long-wavelength red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) using a simple solvothermal method with glycine as the precursor and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen-doping agent. We characterized the structure, surface composition, and optical properties of the synthesized R-CDs. Experiments showed that the prepared R-CDs exhibit good salt resistance and photostability, with a quantum yield of up to 26.7 %. Based on the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots, we developed a system for rapid and quantitative detection of ceftazidime. The fluorescence quenching intensity showed a good linear relationship with CF concentration in the range of 0–0.7 mmol/L, with a linear correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9945 and a detection limit as low as 4.9 μmol/L. This research demonstrates significant advantages in sensitivity, selectivity, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness, opening new avenues for the development of long-wavelength carbon dots in antibiotic detection.

Glycine, o-phenylenediamine, L-ascorbic acid, glutamic acid, and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. Amoxicillin was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Phenylalanine, histidine, oxytetracycline, and chlortetracycline were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. L-threonine and L-hydroxyproline were purchased from Shanghai Yuanju Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All reagents used were of analytical grade to ensure experimental accuracy. Distilled water was used throughout the experiments. 25 mL hydrothermal reactors were purchased from Xiniuke Technology Co., Ltd. An ultrasonic cleaner was acquired from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. A constant temperature forced air drying oven was obtained from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were measured using a Shunyu Hengping 756 PC UV–visible spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Prism F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a VERIEX 70 FTIR spectrometer.

First, 0.15 g of glycine and 0.432 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol, followed by the addition of 0.21 mL of nitric acid. The solution was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene liner and sonicated for 10 min. The liner was placed in a reaction vessel and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the product solution was centrifuged and dialyzed to remove impurities, resulting in the successful preparation of red carbon dots. The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The relative quantum yield (Φ) of fluorescence is defined as the ratio of the number of photons emitted by the fluorescent material to the number of photons absorbed, and serves as a key indicator for evaluating the performance of fluorescent materials, such as carbon dots. The Φ of carbon dots can be determined using the comparative method, as described by Equation (1):
(1)
Φ X
=
Φ S
×
M X
M S
×
η
X
2
η
S
2
where, the subscript S denotes the standard reference material, typically quinine sulfate dissolved in a 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 solution, which has a well-established Φ of 54 %. The subscript X represents the sample being tested. Φ is the quantum yield, M is the slope of the linear regression line obtained by plotting the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensity against the absorbance of the sample or standard at different concentrations, and η is the refractive index of the solvent used for the sample and reference.

To analyze the structure, surface elements, and chemical states of the prepared R-CDs, TEM, FT-IR, and XPS characterizations were performed. The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) shows that R-CDs exhibit an irregular particulate morphology with an average particle size of approximately 2.5 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphene. The FTIR spectrum of R-CDs is shown in Fig. 2b, where the peak around 3660 cm−1 is attributed to O-H stretching vibration absorption, the peak at 2355 cm−1 corresponds to C=N stretching vibration absorption, and the peak at 1532 cm−1 is associated with C-N stretching vibration absorption. These results indicate that nitrogen was successfully doped into the synthesized R-CDs, which may contain water-soluble functional groups such as –NH2 and –OH. Fig. 2c presents the XPS survey spectrum of the prepared sample, revealing that the R-CDs are composed of C, O, and N, with corresponding contents of 74.27 %, 21.42 %, and 4.27 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be deconvoluted into three characteristic peaks corresponding to C-C/C=C (284.58 eV), C-N (285.02 eV), and C-O (288.47 eV). As shown in Fig. 2e, the N1s fine spectrum can be divided into three peaks associated with C-N-C (399.25 eV), N-C (400.4 eV), and N-H (401.3 eV). Fig. 2f shows the O1s fine spectrum, with the peak corresponding to C-O (532.5 eV). These results collectively demonstrate that the prepared R-CDs surface contains abundant nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups.

UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, excitation spectroscopy, and emission spectroscopy were employed to characterize the optical properties of R-CDs. Fig. 3 a shows the three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix of R-CDs, while Fig. 3b presents its two-dimensional projection. It can be clearly observed that the excitation region of R-CDs is mainly located between 360–590 nm, with the emission region primarily between 500–700 nm. Fig. 3c displays the UV–Vis absorption spectrum alongside the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. The maximum excitation peak of R-CDs is at 533 nm, with the maximum emission wavelength at 600 nm. Fig. 3d shows the emission spectra of R-CDs under excitation wavelengths from 510 nm to 560 nm. Notably, the emission wavelength of R-CDs remains unchanged under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3e, after normalizing these emission spectra, it becomes evident that the emission wavelengths almost overlap, indicating that the R-CDs' emission wavelength is independent of the excitation wavelength. This suggests a potentially simple or unified electronic transition mechanism, allowing the carbon dots to emit stable and consistent light under various excitation conditions, which imparts high flexibility and reliability in optical applications.

To evaluate the optical stability of R-CDs under various environments, we investigated the effects of temperature, time, and salt concentration on fluorescence intensity, measured at an excitation wavelength of 533 nm. The salt resistance of R-CDs was studied by maintaining a consistent concentration of R-CDs while adjusting NaCl concentrations to 0, 100 mM, 200 mM, 300 mM, 400 mM, and 500 mM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the NaCl concentration had no significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots, demonstrating stable luminescence even in high-salinity environments. To assess photostability, the carbon dot solution was continuously irradiated with UV light for 100 min. Fig. 4b shows that the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs solution remained almost unchanged, maintaining strong fluorescence intensity throughout the exposure. Temperature stability was explored by measuring fluorescence intensity at 25 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C, and 70 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs solution changed minimally as temperature increased, maintaining stable luminescence. Additionally, we investigated the change in fluorescence intensity of carbon dots stored at room temperature under ambient light for two weeks. As shown in Fig. 4d, the R-CDs maintained strong fluorescence intensity with negligible changes after two weeks of storage. In summary, the synthesized R-CDs exhibit excellent salt resistance and stability.

We further investigated the influence of different solvent systems on the quantum yield of R-CDs. Fluorescence spectra of carbon dots were measured in various solvents including methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The integrated fluorescence intensity was then plotted against absorbance, with results shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b. Using quinine sulfate (REF) as a reference, a steeper slope of the fitted line indicates a higher quantum yield. From the graph, it can be concluded that R-CDs exhibit the highest quantum yield of 26.7 % in ethanol solvent.

Due to their excellent photoluminescence properties, abundant surface functional groups, and outstanding chemical stability, R-CDs have emerged as promising candidates for sensing materials. To investigate their potential, we studied the selectivity of R-CDs by exploring the effects of various metals, antibiotics, and amino acids on their fluorescence intensity. We tested K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, chloramphenicol (CPL), oxytetracycline (OTC), procaine (PRO), amoxicillin (AMC), L-ascorbic acid (ASA), aureomycin (CTC), tetracycline (ACM), L-hydroxyproline (Hyp), histidine (His), phenylalanine (PHE), urea, L-threonine (Thr), glutamic acid (Glu), and ceftazidime (CF). As shown in Fig. 6 a, it is evident that CF significantly quenches the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs, indicating good selectivity of R-CDs for CF.

Fig. 6b demonstrates that as the concentration of CF increases, the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs' dual peaks decreases correspondingly. When the CF concentration is within the range of 0–0.7 mmol/L, both emission peaks show a good linear relationship between (F 0-F)/F 0 and CF concentration (where F is the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots after adding different concentrations of CF, and F 0 is the blank value). Fig. 6c shows the standard curve for the emission peak at 600 nm, with the linear equation (F 0-F)/F 0=0.9564C CF(mmol/L)+0.0089 and a linear correlation coefficient R 2=0.9944. Fig. 6d presents the standard curve for the emission peak at 649 nm, with the linear equation (F 0-F)/F 0=0.6267 C CF(mmol/L)+0.0028 and a linear correlation coefficient R 2=0.9945. Based on 3δ/k (where δ is the standard deviation of six blank samples, and k is the slope of the linear equation), the lowest detection limit of this method was calculated to be 4.9 μmol/L. Therefore, this method can be used for CF detection.

To validate the feasibility of the method, we detected CF content in actual water samples (lake water) and conducted recovery experiments. As shown in Table 1 , the measured recovery rates ranged from 94.2 % to 100.5 %, with relative standard deviations between 2.1 % and 3.2 %. This indicates that R-CDs can be used for the determination of CF in real samples.

To further elucidate the mechanism of CF-induced fluorescence quenching in R-CDs, we first measured the UV absorption spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs+CF mixtures. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the absorption peaks of R-CDs (black) are mainly located around 240 nm and 270 nm. After adding CF to the R-CDs solution, the absorption spectrum (red) showed no significant changes in the absorption peaks, suggesting that the cause of fluorescence quenching might be dynamic quenching. We also measured the excitation spectrum of R-CDs and the UV absorption spectrum of CF, as shown in Fig. 7b, and found no overlap between the two, indicating the absence of an inner filter effect (IFE). Finally, we measured the fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs and R-CDs+CF, as shown in Fig. 7c. The fluorescence lifetimes changed, with calculated values of 1.8 ns and 3.4 ns for R-CDs and R-CDs+CF, respectively. Therefore, the quenching of R-CDs fluorescence by CF might be the result of dynamic quenching.

In summary, we successfully prepared long-wavelength red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) using a solvothermal method, with glycine as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen-doping agent. The structure and optical behavior of the synthesized R-CDs were characterized using infrared absorption spectroscopy, UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs were found to be 533 nm and 600 nm, respectively, with R-CDs exhibiting single-emission behavior. The prepared R-CDs demonstrated good salt resistance and stability, with the highest quantum yield of 26.7 % in ethanol. Based on their fluorescence quenching effect, we established a rapid detection method for ceftazidime. The fluorescence quenching intensity showed a good linear relationship with CF concentration in the range of 0–0.7 mmol/L, with the linear equation (F 0-F)/F 0=0.9564C CF(mmol/L)+0.0089 and a linear correlation coefficient R 2=0.9945. The detection limit was 4.5 μmol/L, with recovery rates ranging from 94.2 % to 100.5 %. The R-CDs synthesized in this study demonstrate excellent performance in terms of selectivity, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness for CF detection. This research provides new ideas and methods for the development of long-wavelength carbon dots in the field of antibiotic detection.
#These authors contributed equally and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.N.J.I. Edison, R. Atchudan, M.G. Sethuraman, J.-J. Shim, Y.R. Lee, Microwave assisted green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots: cytotoxicity and bio-imaging applications, Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology, 161 (2016) 154-161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective and sensitive chemosensor for lead ions using fluorescent carbon dots prepared from chocolate by one-step hydrothermal method
Sens. Actuators B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, Q. Zhou, J. Li, M. Lei, X. Yan, Selective and sensitive chemosensor for lead ions using fluorescent carbon dots prepared from chocolate by one-step hydrothermal method, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 237 (2016) 597-604.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots and their applications
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.V. de Medeiros, J. Manioudakis, F. Noun, J.-R. Macairan, F. Victoria, R. Naccache, Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots and their applications, Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 7 (2019) 7175-7195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile large-scale microwave synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from benzenediol isomers
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, C. Cheng, Y. Huang, B. Zheng, H. Yuan, L. Bo, M.-W. Zheng, S.-Y. Yang, Y. Guo, D. Xiao, A facile large-scale microwave synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from benzenediol isomers, Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 2 (2014) 5028-5035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “Self-heating” enabled one-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots
Eng. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ma, W. Zhong, J. Zhao, S.L. Suib, Y. Lei, “Self-heating” enabled one-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots, Engineered Science, 9 (2019).
